whatThisIs: A bedside reference for therapeutic drug monitoring of the drugs that most often get a level drawn on an inpatient service: vancomycin (trough or AUC), digoxin, lithium, phenytoin (total and free), valproate, tacrolimus, gentamicin (peak / trough), and theophylline. Sophie Well shows the target range, the conventional sampling time relative to the dose (true trough vs. random level, mid-dose vs. immediately before the next dose), and a one-line clinical note on how to act on a value outside the range.
whenToUse: Use this when ordering or acting on a drug level - to verify the level was drawn at the right time relative to the dose (a vancomycin trough drawn an hour after the last dose is not a trough; a phenytoin level on a hypoalbuminemic patient needs a free level, not a total), to confirm the institutional target matches the conventional range, and to translate a result into a dose adjustment direction. The ranges shown here are FDA labeling and standard pharmacology; institutional protocols, assay-specific corrections, indication-specific targets (e.g., vancomycin AUC 400-600 in severe MRSA), and patient-specific factors (renal function, drug interactions, pregnancy) override the generic ranges.
